content.subtitle: Form
items: Countable nouns exist in both a singular and a plural form. ✓ That **sculpture** looks incredible. ✓ Those **sculptures** are quite mediocre.
A number of countable nouns form their plural irregularly, eg person/people, mouse/mice.
Certain countable nouns remain identical in both singular and plural forms, eg the **sheep** is ..., the **sheep** are ...

content.subtitle: Hyphenated nouns and groups
items: When a countable noun is hyphenated, the plural is generally formed by adding -s to the key word, eg brothers-in-law and over-achievers.
When referring to organisations or collectives (eg group/team/etc), both a singular and a plural verb are often equally acceptable. ✓ The government is/are not doing anything to help the arts.

content.subtitle: Collective phrases
content.description: To refer to a group of certain countable nouns, specific phrases with of are used. These include:
items: a flock of birds/sheep
a herd of cows/elephants
a pack of cards/dogs
a bunch of flowers/grapes/keys
a set of encyclopaedias/keys

content.subtitle: Characteristics
items: Singular uncountable nouns exist only in a singular form and always require a singular verb. ✓ Is the information reliable?

content.subtitle: Common examples
items: advice, blood, bread, furniture, hair, information, jewellery, knowledge, luggage, milk, money, news, permission, respect, water

content.subtitle: Describing a particular item
content.description: To refer to a specific quantity or item of a singular uncountable noun, a phrase with of is required before the noun. Frequently used phrases include:
items: a bar of chocolate/soap
a bit of help/advice
a blade of grass
a block of concrete
a breath of fresh air
a drop of water
a grain of salt/sand
a gust of wind
a loaf of bread
a lump of sugar
a piece of bread/information
a scrap of paper
a sheet of paper
a slice of bread/cheese
a speck of dust/dirt
a spot of ink

content.subtitle: Characteristics
items: Plural uncountable nouns only appear in a plural form and always take a plural verb. ✓ The **scissors aren't** on the table.

content.subtitle: Common examples
items: arms, binoculars, cattle, clothes, congratulations, earnings, glasses, goods, groceries, jeans, odds, pants, pliers, premises, pyjamas, regards, remains, savings, scales, scissors, shorts, surroundings, thanks, tights, trousers, valuables

content.subtitle: Phrases
items: Plural uncountable nouns can sometimes be preceded by a pair of, particularly when the item is conceived as having two halves or legs, eg a pair of binoculars/trousers/scissors/etc.

sections.title: Nouns with different meanings and uses

content.subtitle: Watch out!
items: Some uncountable nouns end in -s but are singular, eg diabetes, news, physics, politics.
Many nouns are countable with one meaning and uncountable with another meaning. These include: cake, chicken, chocolate, damage, glass, hair, paper, time, wood, work
✓ The table is made of wood. (uncountable, = the material)
✓ It's a picture of a local wood. (countable, = a small forest)
Some nouns which are usually uncountable are used as countable nouns in certain expressions, eg a knowledge of, a great help.

content.subtitle: Quantifiers only used with countable nouns
items: a couple of (the), a number of, another (of the), both (of) (the), each (of the), either (of the), every, neither (of the), the entire, the whole (of) (the), (a) few (of the), only a few (of the), half (of) (the), many (of the), several (of the)

content.subtitle: Quantifiers only used with singular uncountable nouns
items: an amount of, a great deal of, a little (of the), little (of the), much (of the), only a little (of the)

content.subtitle: Quantifiers used with all nouns
items: all (of) (the), a lot of / lots of (the), all (of) (the), any (of the), enough (of the), more (of the), most (of the), no, none (of the), plenty of (the), some (of the)

content.subtitle: Note
items: a few = some; few = not many; only a few = not many
little + countable noun = small; little + uncountable noun = not much; a little + uncountable noun = some

content.subtitle: Uses
items: Referring to one thing without specifying which one
Introducing something for the first time in a conversation
Speaking about things in general terms (formal register)
Indicating the category or classification that someone or something belongs to.

content.subtitle: Examples
items: I'd like to go to a concert tonight but there's nothing good on.
I've had a great idea!
A poet sees the world differently. (= Poets see …)
Liz is a modernist.

content.subtitle: Watch out!
items: We use a before a consonant sound, and an before a vowel sound. It is the sound and not the spelling that is important (eg a unique experience, an umbrella).

content.subtitle: Uses
items: Referring to something specific or already known
Speaking in general terms (formal register)
With physical things that are unique (eg the moon, the Queen)
With superlatives (eg the best) and cardinal numbers (eg the first)
To mean the well-known or the famous.

content.subtitle: Examples
items: Is that the band you were talking about?
The guitar is one of the oldest musical instruments. (= Guitars are ... )
The scales are balanced to symbolise equality.
Who did the publicity for the show?
Pop music has always appealed more to the young than the old.
I bumped into Damian Hirst, the artist, in the supermarket. (= the well-known artist)

sections.title: Zero article: no article at all

content.subtitle: Uses
items: With plural countable and uncountable nouns when talking generally
With singular uncountable nouns when talking generally
For concepts (ie not physical things), eg society, space, nature.

content.subtitle: Examples
items: Don't let your young child use scissors unsupervised.
An artist always needs inspiration.

sections.title: Articles by category and with particular phrases and expressions

content.subtitle: Time
items: **a/an**: in an hour, in a second
**the**: in the 1840s, in the winter, in the afternoon
**zero**: in 2010, in winter, in December, on Tuesday, at night

content.subtitle: People and work
items: **a/an**: have a job, work as a teacher, I met a very nice American last night
**the**: the King, the Principal, the President, the British
**zero**: Russians, become President, go to work, be at work, have work to do

content.subtitle: Places
items: **a/an**: Is there a beach near here?
**the**: the Himalayas, the Pacific Ocean, the Seine, the Earth, the Antarctic, the USA, the UK, the Scilly Isles
**zero**: Mount Everest, Berlin, America, Antarctica, Jupiter, Fleet Street, Lake Michigan, Mykonos

content.subtitle: Public buildings
items: **a/an**: Is there a bank near here?
**the**: the bank, the post office, go to the hospital/prison/school (as a visitor)
**zero**: go to school/hospital/prison (as a student/patient/prisoner)

content.subtitle: Entertainment and sport
items: **a/an**: Play us a song!, I've got a tennis ball.
**the**: play the guitar, the media, on the radio, go to the cinema, watch the TV
**zero**: play tennis, play guitar, listen to music, on television, watch TV

content.subtitle: Organisations
items: **a/an**: Does Switzerland have an army?
**the**: the BBC, the police, the emergency services, the United Nations
**zero**: NATO

content.subtitle: Education
items: **a/an**: have a lesson, take an exam
**the**: be in the first year
**zero**: geography, be in class/year/form 5

content.subtitle: Travel
items: **a/an**: take a taxi, catch a bus/train
**the**: in the car/taxi, on the bus/plane
**zero**: on foot, go home, go by car/plane

content.subtitle: Health
items: **a/an**: have a cold/cough/headache/toothache/stomach ache
**the**: have the flu/measles
**zero**: have flu/measles/toothache/stomach ache

sections.title: A: Countable or uncountable?
sections.instructions: Circle the correct word. If both are correct, circle both.

items.sentence: We spotted several **sheep / sheeps** grazing by the riverside this morning.
Both of my **sister-in-laws / sisters-in-law** are coming to visit next weekend.
She pointed out three beautiful **fish / fishes** swimming near the surface of the pond.
There's only one piece of **new / news** I need to share with the whole team.
After the haircut, the barber brushed all the **hair / hairs** from the cape.
The receptionist will hand out **information / informations** sheets before the tour begins.
We need to replace most of the **furniture / furnitures** in the waiting room.
You'll need written **permission / permissions** from your employer to attend the conference.
**Physics / Physics** is apparently her strongest subject at secondary school.
Did you manage to find enough **money / monies** to cover the repair costs?
Could you please pass on a bit of **advice / advices** to the newer members of staff?
The exhibition featured some truly remarkable antique **jewellery / jewelleries**.
Would you like some **chocolate / chocolates**?
You need a thorough **knowledge / knowledges** of local history to answer this correctly.
She admits she has never been particularly passionate about **politic / politics**.

sections.title: B: Subject-verb agreement
sections.instructions: Complete using the correct form of the verb in brackets.

items.sentence: ...... (be) all the furniture in the new flat bought second-hand?
Her monthly earnings ...... (rise) significantly since she got the promotion.
...... (be) all the luggage already loaded onto the coach?
Your pyjamas ...... (be) still in the dryer, I think.
Politics ...... (be) something my grandfather has always been very passionate about.
...... (be) the team ready to take the field?
The people in the queue ...... (seem) quite frustrated by the delay.
...... (be) the news encouraging this time round?
There ...... (be) some blood on the bandage, which concerned the nurse.
The cattle ...... (graze) peacefully in the lower field this afternoon.
Diabetes ...... (affect) millions of people across the globe.
Where ...... (be) the scissors I left on the worktop?
His work ...... (be) consistently of the highest standard.
I think the milk ...... (turn) sour. You should check before using it.
The jeans she ordered online ...... (be) completely the wrong size.

sections.title: C: Collective phrases
sections.instructions: If a word in bold is correct, put a tick. If it is in the wrong sentence, write the correct word on the line.

items.sentence: She stopped to buy a **gust** of soap at the pharmacy on the way home.
He reached for a **herd** of playing cards to show the guests a magic trick.
Could I have another **block** of bread with my soup, please?
A **gust** of lemon juice should be enough to add a little acidity to the dish.
I just need a **flock** of fresh air — I'll be back in a moment.
We watched a **pack** of deer grazing quietly at the edge of the forest.
He said he wanted to buy a new **bar** of shorts for the summer.
She asked me to grab a **slice** of bread from the bakery on the way home.
He bought a **blade** of tulips to brighten up the dining table.
A sudden **gust** of wind knocked over all the chairs on the terrace.
There wasn't a **breath** of dirt on the windowsill after she had finished cleaning.
Could you cut me a thin **slice** of ham to go with the salad?
An enormous **grain** of geese flew overhead in perfect formation.
Not a single **loaf** of grass was left after the rabbits had been through the garden.
We always enjoy a nice **bit** of roast chicken on Sundays, don't we?
That **sheet** of ice in the car park is going to be very difficult to remove.
Here's a **set** of keys — the gold one is for the main entrance.
There isn't a **grain** of doubt in my mind that he is telling the truth.
Is one **piece** of sugar really enough to sweeten that amount of tea?
Let me tear off a **lump** of paper so you can write the address down.

sections.title: D: Nouns with different meanings
sections.instructions: Complete the pairs of sentences using the correct form of a word in the box.

items.sentenceA: Right! I've got a lot of ...... to get through before the end of the day.
items.sentenceB: Several of his ...... have been acquired by private collectors.

items.sentenceA: The flooding caused significant ...... to the ground floor of the building.
items.sentenceB: The court ordered the company to pay ...... of over €500,000.

items.sentenceA: She managed to bake four ...... in a single afternoon for the charity sale.
items.sentenceB: Would you like another slice of ...... ?

items.sentenceA: You've been an absolute ...... to us over the past few months.
items.sentenceB: Without your ...... , we honestly don't know what we would have done.

items.sentenceA: He has noticed a few grey ...... recently and is not at all happy about it.
items.sentenceB: Long ...... is apparently back in fashion, according to the magazines.

items.sentenceA: Interestingly, ...... is technically classified as an amorphous solid.
items.sentenceB: Has anyone seen my reading ...... ? I think I left them on the kitchen table.

items.sentenceA: She broke her right ...... in a skiing accident last winter.
items.sentenceB: The country has been accused of selling ...... to rebel groups.

items.sentenceA: They spent the whole morning walking through the ...... behind the old farmhouse.
items.sentenceB: The frame is made entirely of ...... , not metal.

items.sentenceA: Her ...... of Renaissance architecture is quite extraordinary.
items.sentenceB: A basic ...... of first aid could save someone's life one day.

items.sentenceA: Scientists have long dreamed of travelling beyond ...... .
items.sentenceB: There are very few parking ...... left anywhere near the town centre.

items.sentenceA: Before ...... was widely available, important records were carved into stone tablets.
items.sentenceB: She reads three different Sunday ...... every week without fail.

items.sentenceA: One of our ...... has been getting out of the enclosure on a regular basis.
items.sentenceB: This ...... soup is absolutely delicious — what is the secret ingredient?

items.sentenceA: How many ...... have I asked you not to leave the door open?
items.sentenceB: What ...... does the last train to the city depart tonight?

items.sentenceA: Let's pick up a large bar of dark ...... for the recipe.
items.sentenceB: There are only three ...... left in the assortment box, I'm afraid.

sections.title: E: Quantifiers
sections.instructions: Complete each second sentence using one of the two words in bold and any other words you need so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence.

items.sentence: Only two students raised their hands during the entire lesson.
Quite a few residents complained about the noise from the construction site.
Back then, I had no experience of working within large organisations.
There's not much fuel left in the tank.
A handful of volunteers have already put their names down for the event.
I'm sorry, but I barely have any cash on me right now.
I couldn't follow a large portion of what the speaker was saying.
Not enough young professionals are choosing careers in the public sector.

sections.title: F: Quantifiers
sections.instructions: Circle the correct word or phrase. More than one option may be correct in each sentence.

items.sentence: Would you like **any / some** tea?
I've barely got **any / some** time left!
**Only a few / Few / A few** of the seats are still available.
I've only got a **little / little / a small amount of** patience, so be quick!
items.answer: a little / a small amount of

items.sentence: **Neither / Neither of the / Both** candidates was shortlisted for the final round.
**All / Most / Many / Much / Both** of the documents had been shredded.
**None / Half / Whole / Each / Every** of the cups had been placed upside down.
**Each / Every / All** participant whose response was incomplete, please try again.
There's **a little / little / not much / not many** we can do to reverse the situation, unfortunately.
We've still got **most / plenty / a lot / lots / lot** of work to do before the deadline.

sections.title: G: Articles
sections.instructions: Complete the sentences with a, an or the. If no article is required, put a dash (-).

items.sentence: I believe there's ………… pharmacy open on ………… high street somewhere.
One of ………… delegates I met at ………… conference ………… last week was ………… architect at quite ………… well-known firm.
………… reliable dictionary is ………… essential tool for any serious student.
Who arranged ………… flowers for ………… graduation ceremony?
………… young and ………… elderly are often the groups most affected by ………… public spending cuts.
When I was at ………… secondary school, we had to wear ………… tie until we were ………… 16 and then we were allowed to wear ………… open-collar shirt.
I bumped into ………… novelist ………… Amara Singh ………… other day. She gave me ………… signed copy of her ………… latest novel.
I genuinely believe that ………… bicycle is one of ………… most underrated inventions in ………… history of ………… world.
Was that ………… emu or ………… eagle?
What's ………… difference between ………… mirage and ………… oasis? You don't know? Then I'm not sure I can trust you on ………… geography quiz!
Is that sculpture really carved entirely out of ......?
Let's play cards. Have we got a ...... somewhere?
Luckily, only ...... of the paint was actually wasted.
The ...... on the shop floor.
Hardly ...... of the applications received met the minimum criteria.
What ...... opportunity to witness the Northern Lights in person!
You can't simply buy ...... — it has to be given freely.
Almost ...... employee who attended the training said it had been valuable.

sections.title: I: Text completion
sections.instructions: Complete the text with a, an, the or the correct quantifier from the options given.

items.sentence: In (1) ...... field of professional photography, (2) ...... photographer typically earns income through licensing agreements.
For (3) ...... image that is published commercially, they are entitled to receive a set fee.
The more established the photographer, the greater their bargaining power, and (4) ...... higher the rates they can demand.
Although financial uncertainty is common in this field, a skilled photographer can earn a (5) ...... deal of money from a single high-profile commission.
You only have to look at (6) ...... fashion photographer Annie Leibovitz to see that.
For (7) ...... illustrator, however, the financial situation is considerably more complex.
An illustrator sells their artwork for (8) ...... flat fee.
(9) ...... time that piece is reprinted or licensed, its commercial value grows.
The illustrator does not, however, receive (10) ...... additional income from these subsequent uses.
More than a (11) ...... illustrators today, though, are fully aware of this disparity, and so they practise what is sometimes called 'selective licensing'.
They may produce, say, ten illustrations for an exhibition, but only allow a certain (12) ...... of them, say eight, to be sold immediately.
The remaining two they keep back, in (13) ...... hope that their value will increase substantially over time.
Since (14) ...... illegal copying of digital artwork via online platforms has become so widespread,
(15) ...... illustrators and photographers have grown deeply concerned that their primary income stream will be undermined.

Certainly, the (16) ...... creative industry — be it photography, illustration or graphic design — is under immense pressure, but there is still a vast (17) ...... of money to be made from original commissions.
